cards.back: Air scatters shorter blue wavelengths more (Rayleigh scattering).
Light is far faster than sound — ~3 s delay per km.

cards.front: How does a microwave heat food?
cards.back: It vibrates water molecules; watery parts warm first.

cards.front: Why do onions make you cry?
cards.back: Cutting releases a sulfur gas that irritates the eyes.
Water expands when frozen, so ice is less dense than liquid water.
Earth's tilt, not its distance from the sun.
It does — but moves sideways fast enough to keep missing (an orbit).

cards.front: Why does metal feel colder than wood?
cards.back: It conducts heat from your hand faster, at the same temperature.
Rivers carry dissolved minerals to the sea, concentrated by evaporation.
Raindrops bend and split sunlight into colors at a fixed angle.
No — in a vacuum a feather and a hammer fall together; air resistance causes the difference.
Earth spinning on its axis, not the sun moving around us.
How much of the sunlit side faces us — not Earth's shadow.
The Moon's gravity pulling on the oceans (the Sun helps too).
Sound needs a medium; space is near-vacuum, so it can't travel.
Evaporating water carries heat away from your skin.
Yeast eats sugar and releases CO2 bubbles that get trapped in the dough.
Iron reacting with oxygen and water over time forms iron oxide.
Plants use sunlight to turn CO2 and water into sugar, releasing oxygen.
Built-up electrons jumping to balance charge between you and an object.
Light bending through hot air near the ground looks like a reflective puddle.

cards.front: Why do we get a fever?
cards.back: A higher temperature helps the immune system fight infection — it's a defense.

cards.front: Are red or blue stars hotter?
cards.back: Blue stars are hotter; red are cooler — color reveals temperature.
